3.happening: self.val = val stores the integer on this node.
3.why: Each ListNode holds its value in self.val; this is set during construction for every node we create.
3.invariant: This node's val field is now populated with the integer value.

4.happening: self.next = nxt sets the next pointer (None by default).
4.why: The linked list is formed through .next pointers; None marks the tail of the list.
4.invariant: This node's next field is initialized — nodes are disconnected until .next is explicitly set.

8.happening: First call only: head arrived as a Python list — detect and convert it.
8.why: The tracer passes the preset input as a plain Python list; we need real ListNode objects for genuine recursion.
8.invariant: After the if-block, head is a real ListNode or None.

9.happening: Save the raw integer values as vals.
9.why: We use vals to construct the nodes in the next two lines.
9.invariant: vals is the input integer list.

10.happening: Create one ListNode for each integer in vals.
10.why: Build the node objects before chaining them.
10.invariant: nodes_list is a flat list of disconnected ListNodes.

11.happening: Iterate to wire up .next pointers between consecutive nodes.
11.why: Without .next connections the nodes are isolated.
11.invariant: Each node will be linked to the next after this loop.

12.why: This is what makes it a linked list.

13.happening: head is set to the first node (or None for an empty input).
13.why: Now head is a proper ListNode ready for the recursive helper.
13.invariant: head is a real ListNode or None.

14.happening: Call _recurse(head, val) — the pure recursive helper that returns a ListNode.
14.why: Delegating to _recurse keeps the result type consistent (always a ListNode or None, never a list).
14.invariant: new_head is the first surviving node, or None if all nodes were removed.

15.happening: Initialise result = [] to collect surviving values.
15.why: The platform expects a Python list as output, not a ListNode.

16.happening: node starts at new_head — the first surviving node after all deletions.
16.why: We walk from the new head to the end to collect values.
16.invariant: node is the current position in the result collection walk.

17.happening: Check: is node non-null? If yes, there is another surviving value to collect.
17.why: Continue until we reach the end of the cleaned list.
17.invariant: node is non-null while there are more surviving nodes.

18.why: Collect this surviving node's integer value into the output list.
18.invariant: result grows by one entry per surviving node.

19.why: Move to the next surviving node.
19.invariant: node advances one step through the cleaned list.

20.happening: Return result — the ordered list of surviving values.
20.why: The recursion is done; result contains exactly the values of nodes whose value ≠ val.
20.invariant: Every node with value == val has been removed.

23.happening: head is None — end of list reached. This is the base case of the recursion.
23.why: Every recursive path must terminate. An empty tail means there is nothing left to remove.
23.invariant: We will return None to signal an empty tail to the caller.

23.happening: head is not None — there is a real node here to process.
23.why: We have not hit the end yet; continue recursing deeper.
23.invariant: head is a real node whose value will be compared to val after the tail is cleaned.

24.happening: Return None — empty tail for this base case.
24.why: The caller will set its head.next to None, marking the end of the cleaned list.

25.happening: Recurse into head.next to clean the tail first.
25.why: The recursive strategy is bottom-up: fix the tail before deciding what to do with the current node. After this line, head.next is the already-cleaned tail.
25.invariant: head.next is now the cleaned tail — no val-matching nodes remain beyond this point.

26.happening: head.val equals val — this node must be deleted!
26.why: The algorithm removes every node whose value matches val.
26.invariant: We will skip this node by returning head.next.

26.happening: head.val does NOT equal val — this node survives.
26.why: Only val-matching nodes are removed; all others stay.
26.invariant: We will return head to include this node in the result.

27.happening: Return head.next — skip this node entirely.
27.why: By returning head.next instead of head, the caller never sees this node.
27.invariant: The deleted node is now unreachable from the result chain.

28.why: head.val ≠ val; this node belongs in the output. Its .next already points to the cleaned tail.
28.invariant: head is the local root of the cleaned sub-list.

init.happening: Converting the input list to real ListNode objects.
init.why: The recursive helper needs real .next pointers to follow.
init.invariant: head is a real ListNode or None.

check.happening: Checking whether head is None (base case) or head.val equals val.
check.why: The base case stops recursion; the val check decides keep or skip.
check.invariant: The decision point for each frame.

recurse.why: Process the tail before deciding what to do with the current node.
recurse.invariant: After recursion, head.next is the already-cleaned tail.

done.happening: Returning either None (base), head.next (skip), or head (keep).
done.why: Each recursive frame hands its clean sub-result back up the call stack.
done.invariant: The returned node (or None) contains no val-matching nodes in its chain.

loop.happening: Walking the cleaned list to collect result values.
loop.why: The platform expects a list of integers, not a ListNode.
loop.invariant: result grows with each surviving node.

update.happening: Appending a surviving node's value to result.
update.why: Collect the integer for the output list.
update.invariant: result contains values seen so far.

return.happening: Returning the complete list of surviving values.
return.why: All deletions are done; result is the final answer.
return.invariant: No node with value == val remains.
